summary: Gruber connects Apple CFO Peter Oppenheimer's cryptic mention of a 'product transition' during a July 2007 earnings call to the subsequent iPod Touch launch and iPhone price cut. He methodically eliminates other product announcements (Shuffle, iTunes, Nano, Classic) as causes for the projected earnings decline. His central argument is that Apple is moving additional products—particularly the iPod Touch and possibly the entire new iPod lineup—to subscription-based accounting, spreading revenue recognition over 24 months. This accounting shift, rather than simply lower hardware margins, best explains the projected 30% earnings decline Oppenheimer foreshadowed.
key_points: Oppenheimer's enigmatic 'product transition' comment during Apple's Q3 2007 earnings call pointed to something beyond routine hardware updates
The iPod Touch at $299 likely has significantly lower margins than other iPods, given its multi-touch display, faster processor, and Wi-Fi cost only $100 more than the 8GB Nano
Subscription-based accounting—spreading revenue over 24 months to allow free software updates—is the most likely explanation for the projected earnings drop
The iPod Touch's sparse home screen icons strongly suggest it will receive ongoing software updates like the iPhone, necessitating the same accounting treatment
Apple's earnings guidance is notoriously conservative, so the projected decline may not materialize as severely as stated
money_quotes: Oppenheimer said, there will be a 'product transition I can't get into.'
I think what he's saying is that Apple is going to replace an older product which currently has high margins with a new product that, at least at first, will have significantly lower margins.
If you thought there were glaringly empty spots for additional icons on the iPhone home screen, take a look at the iPod Touch's.
The iPod Touch and iPhone are clearly sibling products running variants of the exact same system software; I expect future software updates to appear simultaneously for both products.
It's also possible that it was at $599 that Apple was booking its usual margin, and that at $399, it's now selling iPhones for a far-lower-than-usual margin in order to build market share.
I say 'supposed' given that Apple's guidance is notoriously pessimistic; exactly no one will be surprised if they come in far ahead of their 65 cents per share guidance.
key_insight: Apple's projected earnings decline was not primarily about hardware margins but about a deliberate shift to subscription-based accounting for the iPod Touch and potentially the entire iPod line, enabling free software updates at the cost of deferred revenue recognition.
